ALEX VANYO: Hi, I'm Alex,
a developer relations

engineer working on
adaptive Android apps.

EMILIE ROBERTS: And I am Emilie
I work on Android on ChromeOS.

And if you haven't heard
the term "adaptive" before,
you are in luck.

We are going to take you
through the why, what,

and how of adaptive
Android development.

We'll talk about why
this is important,

about a whole bunch
of new form factors,

and how many users are using
your apps on multiple devices.

We'll dig a bit deeper into what
exactly the adaptive mindset is.

And we'll look at some new tools
in Compose and Android Studio
to help you build.

We also have an important
Android 16 announcement

that could affect your apps in a
big way, so stay tuned for that.

So let's start with
where we are now

and why it's important, why
it's time to think adaptive.

When smartphones
first appeared, they

were proof of how far
computing had come.

Before the 1960s, computers
could be room-sized boxes full

of vacuum tubes
with punch cards.

And then you could miraculously
carry massive computing power
around in your pocket.

And Android apps
unlock the potential

of those devices for
billions of users.

That's really a paradigm
shift in how many
humans interact with the world.

ALEX VANYO: As more apps and
games have come to market,

users expect them to run well
on all the surfaces they use,

from the watch on their wrists
to the Chromebook laptop

to the screen in
their parked car,

and soon, even
projected into extended
reality from their headsets.

We think it's important
to design and build

apps to meet your users
wherever they are,

and to bring your awesome
experiences to them

in a way that meets their
expectations and delights them.

And adaptive doesn't just
mean basic functionality
in all these devices.

It also embraces people
with multiple devices
in a device ecosystem.

These are users who are
invested in your apps,

both in terms of
time and their money.

So we should offer them
exceptional experiences.

Users with more than
one device are often
the most engaged users.

For example, across six of the
major streaming apps in the US,

users that had tablet and phones
had three times the amount
of usage than phone-only users.

It makes sense to make that
tablet experience cinematic.

Since last year, many more apps
have been making optimizations

to improve their apps experience
across the device landscape.

And your adaptive apps can now
access new device capabilities

as well as they extend to more
classes of Android devices.

First, on both of our
Android-for-cars platforms,

Android Automotive
OS and Android Auto,

users stay connected, get
personalized navigation,

and can listen to music
during every drive.

And starting this year,
adaptive mobile apps

in permitted categories can be
distributed on both platforms
while parked.

This can be the same
mobile app that you

distribute to phones,
foldables, and tablets
with minimal changes.
It's still Android after all.

Both platforms provide an
app-compatibility experience

that ensures users can
complete the critical journeys
within the app.

So this combination of
foldables, tablets, Chromebooks,

and compatible cars brings us to
over 500 million active devices
that your mobile app can run on.

And that number will
continue to rise.

Second, we're expanding
the number of ways

that your app can show up
on large-screen devices.

In Android 16, we've made
significant advancements

in desktop windowing,
where apps run

in fully freeform Windows like
other desktop environments.

And when you plug in a phone or
tablet to an external display,

that desktop
windowing environment

will also appear on the
connected monitor or TV.

This means that phones will also
benefit from these improvements,

on top of over 500
million active devices.

In Android 16, we've been adding
foundational platform support

for connected displays that
will be available in AOSP

in the coming months
in developer preview.

And finally, our newest
platform is Android XR.

Headsets add a new dimension
to Android, literally.

Rather than running on
a traditional screen,

apps are placed into a fully
immersive virtual environment,

where you have a natural sense
of depth, scale, and realism.

Headsets are the first form
factor that Android XR supports.

And we'll share more information
about the developer experience

for glasses and how you can
participate later this year.

Mobile apps form the foundation
of the home space app experience

in XR, as mobile apps
distributed through the Play

Store will automatically
be available in XR.

If you're building for Android,
you're already building for XR.

All of the work that you've
made and continue to make

in optimizing for different
aspect ratios and arbitrarily

resizing directly translates
into a great out-of-the-box
experience in XR.

And that all leads to
the perfect launching pad
to differentiate for XR.

You can add an entry point to
launch into full space, where

your app runs on its own and
has full control of the content
and environment.

You can transport users
to a virtual space,

load 3D graphics in
your living room,

or attach a giant virtual
screen on the wall.

So taken all together,
we have a growing number

of phones, foldables,
tablets, and Chromebooks.

Many of those are capable
of turning into full desktop

environments when
plugged into peripherals.

And cars and XR will form new
opportunities for running apps.

EMILIE ROBERTS: OK,
I'd like to dive

more into what we mean
by this adaptive mindset.

Adaptive is a strategy
for app development

that takes into account the
whole expanding Android device

ecosystem and aims to make your
apps run across it, regardless

of the screen size
or input method.

It has a holistic view of
how your users often own
more than one of these devices.

And lastly, it considers
new novel devices

that haven't even
been created yet

and aims to lay down
strong engineering
foundations for the future.

I do want to reassure
you that you do not

have to rebuild your whole
app to take advantage
of all these new form factors.

And you don't need to build
a brand-new separate app

for each one of these and
have six different versions
of your app.

The mindset that
we're proposing here

is that your one app can
run across all of these form

factors, so phones, tablets,
foldables, Chromebooks,

connected displays,
XR, and auto.
